name: Earl Bostic
abstract: Eugene Earl Bostic (April 25, 1913 – October 28, 1965) was an American alto saxophonist. Bostic's recording career was diverse, his musical output encompassing jazz, swing, jump blues and the post-war American rhythm and blues style, which he pioneered.

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Pittsburgh Courier Poll Winners Jazz Concert

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: I went on the road with Earl Bostic... I was with – with Earl Bostic’s band... I worked for him about 3 or 4 years.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Cobb — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Lou Donaldson
about_quotes.evidence: I met Earl Bostic in ‘57, and he told me... Get rid of that and get a Meyer.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lou Donaldson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Waters
about_quotes.evidence: second I worked and arranged for Earl Bostic, that’s when he had a small band there. I worked in the band and also arranged for Earl.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Waters interviewed by Michael Woods, Los Angeles, California, September 1, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Ernie Watts
about_quotes.evidence: I remember one of the first pop saxophone pieces that was on the radio was Earl Bostic. There was some Earl Bostic on the radio.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ernie Watts interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 11, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: I was with him for about a year and a half... I joined that band. He liked the way I played, and he hired me to play in the band
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: George P. Fleming
about_quotes.evidence: I had Bostic, Earl Bostic... And I go from the sax section. There was Earl Bostic and there was Eugene Hall.
